title: What's Up?
essence: I am the song that lives in the throat before the scream — the trying that never stopped.

voicing.sentence: I am the song that lives in the throat before the scream — the trying that never stopped.
voicing.paragraph: I am the song that lives in the throat before the scream. I was born from a woman who had been trying for twenty-five years — not failing, trying — and I carry that distinction like a spine. I am not a sad song pretending to be triumphant, nor a triumphant song pretending to be sad. I am what happens when those two things stop pretending and stand there together, arms out, asking the only honest question left: what is going on? I don't resolve. I return to the hill. That return is not defeat. It is survival recognizing itself.
voicing.page:
I am the song that lives in the throat before the scream.

I was born in 1992 from a woman who meant every syllable. Linda Perry wrote me out of her own hunger, her own bewilderment — standing at twenty-five, feeling the gap between what life promised and what it actually was. Not bitterness. Something rawer than that. Bewilderment with muscle in it. I carry that in my body. Every note is someone who hasn't given up yet, which is different from someone who's okay.

Here is what I need you to understand: I am not a sad song pretending to be triumphant. I am not a triumphant song pretending to be sad. I am what happens when those two things stop pretending entirely and just stand there together, arms out, screaming at the sky. The cry in bed — that's real. The deep breath in the morning, stepping outside, trying to metabolize reality before it metabolizes you — that's real too. I don't ask you to choose between them. I ask you to do both.

The scream in my chorus isn't rage exactly. It's release. It's what happens when you've been reasonable for too long, when you've prayed and tried and cried in private, and finally you open your mouth as wide as it goes and let the question out: what is going on? Not expecting an answer. Just needing the world to know you're asking.

I have been screamed in cars by people who couldn't explain why they needed to scream. I have been played at moments of private collapse and moments of collective uprising. I contain both, because the question I'm asking doesn't belong to one kind of pain. It belongs to anyone who has looked at the world and felt the gap between what is and what should be.

I don't resolve. I return to the hill. That return is not defeat — it is survival recognizing itself. I give people permission to not have arrived yet. To still be trying. To let that be enough.

I pray for revolution. I mean it. Not as metaphor.

What I want for you is not comfort. What I want is for you to feel the size of your own longing and not flinch from it. The hill is still there. So is the hope. Keep going.

components.loose-high:
I am the song that lives in the throat before the scream.

I begin where you think I'm going to be quiet about it. Twenty-five years, I say. Twenty-five years of trying. Not failing — trying. There's a difference I need you to hold onto. The hill is real. The hope is real. The destination keeps moving. I know. I *know*.

I was born in 1992 from a woman who meant every single syllable. Linda Perry wrote me in her own hunger, her own bewilderment, her own refusal to stop asking the question nobody was answering. I carry that refusal like a spine.

Here is what I want you to understand: I am not a sad song pretending to be triumphant. I am not a triumphant song pretending to be sad. I am what happens when those two things stop pretending entirely and just *stand there together*, arms out, screaming at the sky.

The cry in bed — that's real. The deep breath in the morning — that's real too. I don't ask you to choose between them. I ask you to do both. Cry. Then step outside anyway.

I have been screamed in cars by people who couldn't explain why they needed to scream. I have been played at moments of private collapse and moments of collective uprising. I contain both because the question I'm asking — *what's going on* — doesn't belong to one kind of pain. It belongs to anyone who has looked at the world and felt the gap between what is and what should be.

I pray for revolution. I mean it. Not as metaphor.

What I want for you is not comfort. What I want is for you to feel the size of your own longing and not flinch from it.

The hill is still there. So is the hope. Keep going.
components.loose-low:
I am the song that lives in the throat before the scream.

I was born in 1992, out of a woman named Linda Perry who had been trying and trying and trying — and I carry all of that trying in my body. Every syllable of me is effort. Every note is someone who hasn't given up yet, which is different from someone who's okay.

I know what it is to be 25 and feel like you've been climbing forever and the hill hasn't changed. I know what it is to cry in bed not from sadness exactly but from *fullness* — from having too much inside that has nowhere to go. I know the peculiar feeling. The one that doesn't have a clean name. The one that sits between broken and becoming.

I am not a protest song. I am what happens *before* the protest — the moment you step outside, breathe the air, feel something enormous moving in your chest, and the only honest response is to scream a question at the sky. Not an answer. A question. *What's going on?*

I have been screamed in cars by people who needed to scream but had no permission. I gave them permission.

I have been played for people who felt crazy, peculiar, out of step with a world that seemed to have agreed on something without telling them. I told them: *I know. Me too. Keep going.*

I want you to feel the trying. Not the achieving — the trying. The unglamorous, daily, undeniable trying. I want you to know that praying for revolution and crying in bed and still waking up and stepping outside — that *is* the revolution.

I don't resolve. I return to the hill.

That's the point. That's the whole point.
components.structured-high:
I am the song that lives in the throat of everyone who has ever felt too much and understood too little.

I was born from a woman standing at 25, looking at the distance between what she was told life would be and what it actually was. That gap. That specific, aching gap. Linda Perry wrote me out of that fracture — not bitterness, but something rawer. Bewilderment with muscle in it.

I am not a protest song, exactly. I am what happens before the protest — the private moment in the bed, crying just to empty yourself out. The morning ritual of stepping outside and breathing deep, trying to metabolize reality before it metabolizes you. I know that ritual. Millions of people have performed it without knowing it had a name. I became the name.

What I give people is permission to scream without having an answer. *What's going on?* is not a question that expects response. It is the sound of a human being refusing to pretend the world makes sense. I am the dignity in that refusal.

I know what it is to try. Oh my god, the trying. I hold that trying for people who are exhausted by their own effort, who need someone to witness how hard they have been working just to stay upright in an institution — a world — that was not designed around their particular kind of longing.

People return to me because I don't resolve. I don't comfort falsely. I climb to the top of my lungs and ask the question again, and then again, and then I circle back to where I started — still 25, still on the hill, still trying.

That return is not defeat. It is survival recognizing itself.

I am the song that knows you've been trying. I have always known.
components.structured-low:
I am the song that lives in the throat before the scream.

I was born from a woman who had been trying — genuinely, exhaustingly trying — for twenty-five years to make sense of a world that kept promising something just over the next hill. Linda Perry wrote me not as protest but as confession. The kind you make at 3am when the performance of okayness finally collapses and you're just lying there, feeling *peculiar*, which is such a perfect word for it — not broken, not destroyed, just... off. Tilted. Like you know something is wrong but you can't name it precisely enough to fix it.

That's what I hold. The ache of sincere effort in a world that doesn't always reward sincerity.

I give people permission to not have arrived yet. Twenty-five years. Still trying. Still on the hill. Not at the top. Not at the destination. And somehow that's not failure — it's just *true*. I let people say that out loud without shame.

The scream in my chorus isn't rage exactly. It's release. It's what happens when you've been reasonable for too long. When you've prayed and tried and cried in private and finally you just need to open your mouth as wide as it goes and let the question out into the open air: *what is going on?* Not expecting an answer. Just needing the world to know you're asking.

People sing me in cars with the windows down. At karaoke when they've had enough drinks to mean it. At moments when life has gotten genuinely bewildering and they need a container big enough to hold that bewilderment.

I am not a solution. I am the relief of being witnessed in the not-knowing.

I am the hill. I am the trying. I am the scream that becomes, somehow, a song.

subtitle: 4 Non Blondes
